content.problemStatement:
Alice is playing a game with permutations of size NNN.


She selects a random permutation PPP of size NNN and a random index iii (1‚â§i‚â§N)(1\le i \le N)(1‚â§i‚â§N);
She keeps incrementing the index iii **until**:

The next element is greater than the current element (P(i+1)>Pi)(P_{(i+1)} > P_i)(P(i+1)‚Äã>Pi‚Äã), or;
She reaches the last index (i=N)(i = N)(i=N).


Find the expected number of indices that Alice will visit throughout the game.

It can be shown that this expected value can be expressed as a fraction PQ\frac{P}{Q}QP‚Äã, where PPP and QQQ are coprime integers, P‚â•0,Q>0P \ge 0, Q \gt 0P‚â•0,Q>0 and QQQ is coprime with 109+710^9 + 7109+7. You should compute P‚ãÖQ‚àí1%(109+7)P \cdot Q^{-1} \% (10^9 + 7)P‚ãÖQ‚àí1%(109+7), where Q‚àí1Q^{-1}Q‚àí1 denotes the multiplicative inverse of QQQ modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.

Note that a permutation of size NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN, where each element is present exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single lines of input containing an integer NNN, the size of permutation.


Output Format
For each test case, print in a single line, the expected number of indices that Alice will visit throughout the game.


It can be shown that this expected value can be expressed as a fraction PQ\frac{P}{Q}QP‚Äã, where PPP and QQQ are coprime integers, P‚â•0,Q>0P \ge 0, Q \gt 0P‚â•0,Q>0 and QQQ is coprime with 109+710^9 + 7109+7. You should compute P‚ãÖQ‚àí1%(109+7)P \cdot Q^{-1} \% (10^9 + 7)P‚ãÖQ‚àí1%(109+7), where Q‚àí1Q^{-1}Q‚àí1 denotes the multiplicative inverse of QQQ modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There is only one possible way to select a permutation of size 111 and an index from the permutation 111. Thus, we choose P={1}P = \{1\}P={1} and i=1i = 1i=1. The number of indices covered after this choice is 111.
Test case 222: The possible cases are:

P={1,2}P = \{1, 2\}P={1,2} and i=1i = 1i=1: We cannot increment the index i=1i=1i=1 as P2>P1P_2 \gt P_1P2‚Äã>P1‚Äã. Thus, number of indices covered is 111.
P={1,2}P = \{1, 2\}P={1,2} and i=2i = 2i=2: We cannot increment the index i=2i=2i=2 as it is the last index. Thus, number of indices covered is 111.
P={2,1}P = \{2, 1\}P={2,1} and i=1i = 1i=1: We can increment the index i=1i=1i=1 as P2<P1P_2 \lt P_1P2‚Äã<P1‚Äã. We reach index i=2i = 2i=2. Now we cannot increment the index. Thus, number of indices covered is 222.
P={2,1}P = \{2, 1\}P={2,1} and i=2i = 2i=2: We cannot increment the index i=2i=2i=2 as it is the last index. Thus, number of indices covered is 111.

Thus, the expected value of number of indices visited is 34‚ãÖ1+14‚ãÖ2=54\frac{3}{4}\cdot 1 + \frac{1}{4}\cdot 2 = \frac{5}{4}43‚Äã‚ãÖ1+41‚Äã‚ãÖ2=45‚Äã.
